title: Canonical Rabies PEP Intake Checklist
description: Unified canonical intake checklist merging all questions from the WHO SEARO Rabies PEP Decision Tree and the Ontario (OPH) RPEP Clinician Decision Tool. Every question from both sources is preserved with exact original wording. Semantic duplicates are merged into single canonical questions with source_map and redundancy_group annotations. Additional clinically relevant questions are included with origin 'additional'.

sources.title: WHO SEARO Rabies PEP Clinician Intake Checklist

sections.title: Animal clinical features and risk assessment

questions.text: Has the patient already started rabies PEP for this exposure?
questions.source_phrase: Has the patient already started rabiesPEP for this exposure?

questions.text: What was the date and time of the exposure? (If unknown, provide best estimate or select 'unknown')
questions.rationale: Exact exposure date/time is essential for determining PEP urgency, observation windows, and whether thresholds such as the WHO 10-day rule apply. Both source checklists reference timing indirectly but neither captures the actual date.

questions.text: What was the geographic location where the exposure occurred?
questions.rationale: Geographic location determines regional rabies epidemiology and species-specific risk. ON admin notes reference this as part of a thorough exposure history and provide travel-related guidance for exposures outside Canada.

questions.text: What type of animal was involved?
questions.source_phrase: What type of animal was involved?

questions.text: Can exposure to bat saliva (or neural tissue) via bite, scratch, mucous membrane, or broken skin be ruled out?
questions.source_phrase: Can exposure to bat saliva (or neural tissue) via bite, scratch, mucous membrane, or broken skin be ruled out?

questions.text: Was a bat actually seen?
questions.source_phrase: Was a bat actually seen?

questions.text: Is the bat available for testing?
questions.source_phrase: Is the bat available for testing?

questions.text: Is the history suggestive of a bat?
questions.source_phrase: Is the history suggestive of a bat?

questions.text: Was there only minimal or uncertain contact with the bat?
questions.source_phrase: Was there only minimal or uncertain contact?

questions.text: Was the bat dead?
questions.source_phrase: Was the bat dead?

questions.text: Was the bat fully dried out (desiccated)?
questions.source_phrase: Was the bat fully dried out?

questions.text: Did any of the following occur?

items.label: Bite that penetrated skin or caused bleeding
Bite that did NOT penetrate skin or cause bleeding
Scratch that broke skin or caused bleeding
Scratch that did NOT break skin or cause bleeding
Neural tissue contacting broken skin or mucous membrane

questions.source_phrase: Did ANY of the following occur? - Bite / Scratch that broke skin / Saliva contacting broken skin / Saliva contacting mucosa / Neural tissue contacting broken skin or mucosa
inline_notes: Per WHO, if exposure was to a bat, PEP for WHO wound category III should be started immediately.

questions.text: What is the anatomical site of the wound or exposure?
questions.rationale: Anatomical site determines innervation density (head, neck, face, genitals, hands are high-risk) and guides local RIG infiltration. WHO footnote 2 and q07 reference highly innervated areas; capturing the specific site enables automated high-risk flagging.

questions.text: Is the animal still alive?
questions.source_phrase: Is the animal still alive?
inline_notes: If, during subsequent visits, the animal is still alive and the time since exposure exceeds 10 (or 14 days, depending on the animal and local policies), PEP can be discontinued.

questions.text: Is the victim less than 14 years old?
questions.source_phrase: Is the victim <14 years?

questions.text: Does the victim have multiple or deep wounds?
questions.source_phrase: Does the victim have multiple or deep wounds?

questions.text: Has thorough wound washing with soap and water and antiseptic application been performed?
questions.rationale: WHO footnote 5 states wound washing is 'a life-saving practice especially for immunocompromised patients' and recommends 15 minutes of washing. ON admin notes describe it as 'imperative and probably the most effective procedure in the prevention of rabies'. Neither checklist captures whether this critical first-aid step has been performed.

questions.text: Did the animal show signs of disease consistent with rabies?

questions.text: Did the animal die or disappear within 10 days of the exposure?
questions.source_phrase: Did the animal die or disappear within 10 days of the exposure?

questions.source_phrase: Did the animal bite 2 or more people or animals?

questions.source_phrase: Has the animal been tested for rabies by a WOAH recommended test?

questions.source_phrase: Test positive / Test pending / inconclusive / Test negative

questions.source_phrase: Was the animal provoked to bite /scratch?

questions.source_phrase: Did the patient complete a rabies PEP series in the past?

questions.text: Did the patient receive a valid primary series (from rabies pre-exposure prophylaxis or PEP) with documented adequate serologic response?
questions.source_phrase: Did the patient receive a valid primary series with documented adequate serologic response (≥0.5 IU/mL)?

questions.text: How many valid prior rabies vaccine doses did the patient receive?

questions.source_phrase: Was the dose received between 1 week and 1 month ago?

questions.text: Was the second prior rabies vaccinedose received less than 3 years ago?

questions.text: Is the patient currently immunocompromised?

source_map.on_source_phrase: Is the patient currently immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?

questions.text: Is the patient currently on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?

source_map.on_text: Is the patient currently immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?
source_map.on_source_phrase: Is the patient currently immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?

questions.source_phrase: Is the patient currently moderate-to-severely immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?

questions.text: Was the patient immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine at the time of previous vaccination?
questions.source_phrase: Was the patient immunocompromised at the time of previous vaccination?

questions.text: Was the patient on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine at the time of previous vaccination?
questions.source_phrase: Was the patient immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine at the time of previous vaccination?

questions.text: Is the patient currently pregnant?
questions.rationale: Pregnancy does not contraindicate rabies PEP, but it is clinically relevant for treatment counselling, monitoring, and documentation. WHO and national guidelines note that PEP should not be withheld in pregnancy given the fatal nature of rabies, but the clinician should be aware of pregnancy status.

questions.text: Does the patient have any known allergies to rabies vaccine or rabies immunoglobulin components?
questions.rationale: Allergies to vaccine components (e.g., egg protein in some formulations, neomycin) or to equine/human RIG products may require alternative products or desensitisation protocols. This is a standard safety check before any biologic administration.

questions.text: Is RIG/RmAbs availability limited?
questions.source_phrase: RIG/RmABs availability: limited?
